The backend api/main.py has real, well-implemented hardening (constant-time API key check, restrictive CORS, magic-byte upload validation, atomic index writes, rate limiting) — but the app's own product design exposes missing persons' PII to anyone on the internet with no authentication layer in the frontend, contradicting the project's own written spec.

nodes.name: TerraVE
nodes.type: Web application (Next.js full-stack) — disaster-response infrastructure mapping + AI damage classification platform
nodes.summary: TerraVE is a Next.js 14 + Supabase (PostGIS) hackathon MVP that lets citizens report infrastructure locations and classify post-earthquake structural damage from photos via a Hugging Face vision model, displaying result…
nodes.onePitch: TerraVE convierte los reportes ciudadanos y fotos post-terremoto en un mapa colaborativo en tiempo real con clasificación de daños asistida por IA, para saber rápido qué sigue en pie y qué necesita ayuda urgente.

backend: Next.js middleware (custom rate limiting + header hardening)
stack.orm: Supabase JS client (@supabase/supabase-js) used as a BaaS/PostgREST wrapper; some routes bypass the SDK and call the Supabase REST API directly with fetch + hardcoded anon key headers (app/api/satelital, app/api/poblacion)
stack.pattern: Next.js 14 App Router monolith (server + client) with API routes acting as a thin BaaS backend for Supabase

redFlags: No authentication anywhere; Supabase RLS allows public INSERT with the anon key, enabling anyone to spam or falsify infrastructure/damage data

AI damage classification (life-safety relevant: 'no habitar', 'evacuación inmediata') depends on a free-tier third-party Hugging Face model of unstated/unverified accuracy, with silent fallback to 'no evaluado' if the API fails
No automated tests and no CI pipeline
In-memory rate limiter and regex-based SQL-injection filter in middleware are weak/cosmetic and ineffective in a serverless multi-instance deployment
README documents a .env.example file that does not exist in the repo

Git history (99 commits, mostly .gitkeep/upload) indicates the repo was built via GitHub web upload rather than genuine iterative version control, undermining confidence in real-world testing/iteration
nodes.mergePotential: Could be a strong complementary layer for a shelter-mapping or aid-logistics project that needs structural safety data — its damage-classification + geo endpoints (GeoJSON, population exposure) could feed into a broader coordination platform, but its open-write database and lack of auth would need hardening before integration with systems handling sensitive victim data.

diffusion.angle: Frame as: 'Un mapa vivo del terremoto — la comunidad reporta qué queda en pie, la IA ayuda a priorizar qué inspeccionar primero.' Show live map + report form + AI classification flow as a 30-second screen capture since there's no demo video yet.
gaps: No demo video — a 30-60s screen recording of report -> AI classification -> map update would make this instantly shareable
No screenshots captured for social assets
No real usage data / testimonials (0 stars, 1 contributor, brand new repo)
Landing page shows placeholder stats (dashes) instead of live numbers, which undercuts credibility for donors
Should visibly caveat that AI damage classification is 'apoyo, no reemplaza evaluación de especialistas' in any promo copy to avoid overpromising safety guarantees

findings.title: Unauthenticated mass-assignment PATCH on /api/lugares/[id]
Unauthenticated PATCH on /api/report/[id] lets anyone alter any damage classification
Open unauthenticated geocoding proxy (/api/geocode) with no rate limit

security.reauditNote: Since the last audit, only the /api/report/[id] PATCH endpoint was hardened: it now validates the UUID and whitelists editable fields with a strict zod schema, closing the mass-assignment vector, but it still has zero authentication so anyone can still flip a location's damage classification. /api/lugares/[id] PATCH is completely unchanged — still `update(body)` with no auth and no field whitelist, and the tracking issue confirms this is exploitable against the production RLS. A new in-memory rate limiter and basic SQL-injection regex were added in middleware.ts, but its matcher list only covers /api/classify, /api/report, /api/lugares — /api/geocode is not in the matcher at all, so the open geocode proxy remains completely unprotected (no auth, no rate limit), unchanged from the last audit.

nodes.name: Venemed
nodes.type: Full-stack web application (Next.js) for medical-aid donation matching
nodes.summary: VeneMed is a genuinely production-grade Next.js + Supabase/Drizzle application connecting Venezuelan health centers with donors via time-windowed medical supply requests, featuring real auth (OTP + captcha), an admin mo…
nodes.onePitch: VeneMed conecta centros de salud venezolanos con donantes en tiempo real: piden exactamente lo que necesitan, con fecha límite, y ningún insumo se desperdicia.

stack.orm: Drizzle ORM over Postgres (Supabase), raw `postgres` driver; migrations checked in with drizzle-kit; Supabase client used only for auth, not data access
stack.pattern: Next.js App Router monolith with route groups ((public)/(center)/(admin)), server actions for mutations, route handlers for cron/API

redFlags: Single named contributor (bus factor of 1) despite otherwise high engineering quality
Cron-based expiry relies on external GitHub Actions schedule hitting prod — a missed/disabled workflow silently stops expiry
No visible rate limiting beyond Turnstile captcha on OTP send; other mutation endpoints not obviously rate-limited
nodes.mergePotential: Could serve as a strong standalone medical-aid matching platform for hospitals/clinics post-disaster; could also integrate with a broader Build4Venezuela aid-logistics or shelter-mapping platform by exposing its center/request data via an API for cross-project donor routing.

diffusion.angle: 'Ninguna donación se pierde en el camino' — show the live countdown-timer request board where a health center in Venezuela asks for exactly what it needs (with a deadline), and donors can find and share it in seconds without creating an account.
gaps: No demo video to show the flow end-to-end for social content
No dedicated screenshots/press kit assets prepared
Real-user adoption unverified — visible data (3 centros, 1 solicitud) looks like seed/demo content rather than confirmed real health centers using it live
Single contributor / bus factor of 1 is a risk to flag before heavy promotion commitments

findings.title: RLS disabled on all tables (Supabase Data API exposure) — GitHub issue #26
Cron secret uses non-constant-time comparison — GitHub issue #25
OTP send has no captcha — SMS/email budget exhaustion DoS — GitHub issue #22
(admin) middleware gates on session only, not admin role — GitHub issue #24
Public recordShare action has no rate limit — GitHub issue #23
OTP brute-force protection delegated entirely to Supabase config — GitHub issue #21
Placeholder contact email on /privacidad — GitHub issue #31 (open)

security.reauditNote: The previous audit round (issues #21-#26, filed by another Build4Venezuela participant) is fully addressed in code: RLS deny-all + REVOKE was added in migration 0006_locked_data_api.sql (PR #28, closes #26), the cron bearer-token check now uses crypto.timingSafeEqual with fail-closed semantics (closes #25), Turnstile captcha is wired into every signInWithOtp call (closes #22), and admin pages all call requireAdmin() which independently checks is_platform_admin from the DB (closes #24). A fresh scan of all server actions (registro, editar, gestionar, publicar, aviso, recepcion, moderation) found consistent server-side session-derived authorization, no mass-assignment, and no raw-string SQL interpolation. The only remaining open item is GitHub issue #31: the /privacidad contact email is still the literal placeholder 'contacto@ejemplo.com' in src/app/(public)/privacidad/page.tsx, which is the stated channel for PII-deletion requests on a platform built for an at-risk population — low severity but a real, currently-open gap.

nodes.name: Venezuela Juntos (Missing Persons Facial Recognition)
nodes.type: web app (server-rendered FastAPI + Jinja2) for missing-person/found-person matching via facial recognition
nodes.summary: Venezuela Juntos is a FastAPI + SQLite disaster-relief app that lets families report missing persons and rescuers report found persons, then automatically cross-matches uploaded photos via ArcFace/InsightFace facial-rec…
nodes.onePitch: Venezuela Juntos usa reconocimiento facial con IA para reunir a familias separadas por el desastre — sube una foto, y la tecnología busca la coincidencia.

stack.orm: SQLAlchemy 2.x declarative models (User, MissingPerson, FoundPerson, Match, AuditLog) over SQLite; hand-rolled idempotent migration helper that ALTER TABLE ADD COLUMNs for schema evolution
stack.pattern: monolithic server-rendered FastAPI app, single service, modular Python files (main/auth/database/matching/audit + lib/facerec)

redFlags: No automated tests or CI pipeline at all
SQLite as the only datastore — not viable for multi-instance or high-load production use
No rate limiting or visible CSRF protection on login/register/upload endpoints
Stores raw biometric (facial) data for vulnerable disaster victims with no encryption-at-rest, retention policy, or consent flow disclosed
No HTTPS enforcement (self-acknowledged in README) despite session cookies and biometric data being transmitted
No email/SMS notification on match — users must manually check the site (self-acknowledged limitation)
nodes.mergePotential: Could serve as the biometric-matching backend/module for a broader Build4Venezuela platform (e.g., feeding matches into a shelter-mapping or volunteer-coordination system), or be merged with an SMS/email notification service to close the "no proactive notification" gap it self-identifies.

diffusion.angle: Feature the demo video walkthrough with a clear 'missing person facial-matching for reunification' narrative, framed as a promising hackathon prototype/backend module rather than a live tool people can use today
gaps: No public live demo/URL to click and try — repo-only, so donors/press cannot interact with it directly
No screenshots extracted for social cards
No evidence of real users or real matches made
Biometric privacy/consent story needs a public-facing explanation before wide promotion, given it stores facial data of vulnerable people

findings.title: Public /matches endpoint leaks victim PII and rescuer contact info with no authentication
Public /uploads static mount serves victims' face photos without authentication
All data-entry and matching endpoints are unauthenticated, enabling anonymous PII submission and probe-matching abuse

security.reauditNote: Commit 0079d51 ("Add authentication, per-user isolation, admin console, and photo quality handling") added self-signup accounts, signed-cookie sessions, per-user match scoping, and an authenticated /uploads/{filename} route replacing the public StaticFiles mount, which resolves the two higher-severity findings. The residual low-severity finding is only partially closed: registration is free/unverified (no email confirmation, no CAPTCHA, no rate limiting on /login or /register), so an attacker can still trivially create disposable accounts to submit data or run probe-matching against other users' PII, just with one extra step.

nodes.name: Venezuela Response Hub
nodes.type: Static informational landing page / link directory (no backend)
nodes.summary: Venezuela Response Hub is a single-page, static React (TanStack Start) website that acts as a curated directory of external humanitarian links (missing-persons sites, shelter maps, donation portals, emergency numbers) f…
nodes.onePitch: Venezuela Response Hub: un solo lugar bilingüe con enlaces verificados a búsqueda de personas, refugios, ayuda médica y donaciones para el terremoto de Venezuela — encuentra ayuda real en segundos, no en 20 pestañas.

backend: TanStack Start server entry (SSR only, no API routes)
stack.orm: None — no database of any kind exists in the codebase
stack.pattern: Single-page SSR app (TanStack Start) with one real route (src/routes/index.tsx) rendering a long static component tree; src/server.ts wraps the generated SSR handler purely for error normalization

redFlags: README describes it as a 'humanitarian coordination platform' with 'integrated platforms' (Response Hub, SismoLab) but the code contains none of that — it's a single static page of outbound links
No tests, no CI/CD pipeline despite claiming to be a live production site at vzlaresponsehub.org
No backend or database at all; all 'live' stats (1,430+ dead, 68,900+ missing, 'Join 3,247 helpers') are hardcoded strings in meta tags and JSX, not real data

Trust/verification badges for third-party tools ('verified', 'community', 'unverified') appear to be manually assigned by the developer with no stated verification process, which is risky in a disaster context if a link is inaccurate or unsafe
Large amount of unused shadcn/ui boilerplate (60+ component files) inflating the codebase without corresponding usage
nodes.mergePotential: Could serve as a lightweight 'index/landing page' front door linking to more substantive projects in the hackathon (e.g., a real shelter-mapping or people-finder backend), but has no data model or API to merge/integrate with other projects at a code level — any integration would be purely a link/reference addition.

diffusion.angle: Position it honestly as a curated one-stop directory of vetted humanitarian links (missing persons, shelters, donations, emergency numbers) for Venezuela's earthquake — a fast, zero-cost way for victims/donors to find real help in one place — rather than as a 'coordination platform', since it has no backend or live data.
gaps: No real backend/data layer to back up the 'platform' framing or the hardcoded casualty/helper stats shown on the homepage
No verification process disclosed for the manually-assigned 'verified/community/unverified' trust badges on outbound links
No screenshots asset captured/confirmed separately from live demo
Unclear if it has any real users/testimonials beyond the hardcoded '3,247 helpers' counter

security.reauditNote: Re-confirmed: this repo is a static/SSR marketing landing page (TanStack Start, Lovable-generated) with no database, no API routes, no auth, no forms submitting data, and no secrets or env vars referenced anywhere in src/ or config files. The only dynamic server code is src/server.ts, a generic error-normalizing wrapper around the framework's SSR handler with no data handling. No previous findings existed to carry forward (prior audit also reported risk 'none' with no listed findings), and no security tracking issue exists on GitHub. Nothing exploitable was found in this fresh scan.

nodes.name: VidasVE
nodes.type: web app (PWA) with serverless API backend
nodes.summary: VidasVE (MapaRescate) is a production-oriented missing-persons / disaster reporting map for Venezuela: a Vite/React SPA and an Azure Functions TypeScript API backed by Cosmos DB, with real anti-abuse (proof-of-work, rat…
nodes.onePitch: VidasVE: un mapa colaborativo en vivo donde cualquier persona puede reportar el estado de un edificio o zona afectada en Venezuela, para que familiares en el exterior sepan dónde se necesita ayuda — ya desplegado y funcionando hoy.

stack.orm: Raw Cosmos DB SQL API queries via @azure/cosmos SDK, wrapped in a ReportStore interface with two implementations: CosmosReportStore (prod) and MemoryReportStore (in-memory fallback/tests), selected via env var (COSMOS_ENDPOINT / USE_IN_MEMORY_STORE)
stack.pattern: Serverless functions API + SPA frontend, monorepo with npm workspaces (app/, api/, infra/)

redFlags: Single contributor and very young repo (created 2026-06-26, ~49 commits) — no evidence of load testing or real-world usage yet
Relies on a single Azure subscription's Cosmos free tier (1000 RU/s) noted explicitly as a constraint in README, limiting scale without manual upgrade
No automated/AI content moderation — abuse handling depends on community flags and a manual moderation queue with a shared admin token
No rate-limit/captcha bypass audit or penetration testing evidence beyond unit tests
nodes.mergePotential: Strong candidate for a core people-finder/mapping module in a combined platform; its store interface (ReportStore) and moderation/anti-abuse layer could be adapted or referenced by other teams building similar reporting tools, and its geofenced bbox + priority/status derivation logic is reusable for other disaster-reporting use cases.

diffusion.angle: Technical-credibility angle: 'A production-grade, abuse-resistant crowdsourced missing-persons map for Venezuela earthquake zones — live and open source' aimed at developers, NGOs and donors rather than a viral consumer post (since it has zero real reports so far).
gaps: No demo video
No screenshots on file
No real reports/data yet to show social proof
No branding/press kit assets confirmed

findings.title: External image proxy follows redirects without re-validating the allowlist (SSRF)
Production runs with insecure dev fallbacks for security-critical secrets
Rate limiting keys off unauthenticated x-forwarded-for header
Media upload validation trusts client-declared MIME type only; new uploads publish immediately
Media storage account does not disable shared-key access
Admin moderation auth is a single shared bearer token stored in sessionStorage
CI/CD workflows use unpinned action tags and unpinned deploy CLI

security.reauditNote: This repo already has 8 open, unfixed security-tracking issues (#4-#11, filed 2026-06-30 at the exact commit currently at HEAD, 6838715). I independently re-verified every one against the live code and all are confirmed still present with no remediation commits since filing. I found no additional high-impact issues beyond what's already tracked: auth/authz on all state-changing routes is solid (requireAdmin HMAC-based constant-time check, owner-token verification, PoW challenge + Turnstile captcha, per-field whitelisted parsers with no mass-assignment), PII (contact info, owner tokens) is AES-256-GCM encrypted/HMAC-hashed and stripped from all public API responses via publicReport/publicEvent, Cosmos DB queries are fully parameterized (no injection), and media reads use short-lived user-delegation SAS tokens rather than public blob access.

nodes.name: Vzla Encuentra
nodes.type: web app (FastAPI backend API + minimal static HTML admin/front) with facial recognition ML pipeline
nodes.summary: Reencuentros (leer_rostros) is a production-grade FastAPI facial-recognition service to reunite missing persons with families after disasters: familiares upload a photo to search, rescatistas register found persons, and…
nodes.onePitch: VzlaEncuentra: sube una foto y el reconocimiento facial reúne en minutos a las familias separadas por el terremoto — ya en vivo en vzlaencuentra.com.

frontend: single static frontend/index.html (305 lines) — thin admin/demo UI, not a full SPA
stack.orm: raw SQL via psycopg3 connection pool + pgvector extension, wrapped in per-context Repository classes (PersonaRepository, ReporteRepository, TestimonioRepository); no ORM
stack.pattern: layered / bounded-context architecture: FastAPI routes -> use_cases -> repositories -> Postgres, with a pure domain layer (matching policy, privacy rules, entities) independent of SQL/HTTP

redFlags: No structured logging/observability (only print statements at startup) — will hamper incident response in a real deployment
No visible rate limiting on public endpoints like /admin/login or the search endpoints, leaving some brute-force/DoS exposure
InsightFace runs on CPUExecutionProvider only; will need infra scaling (GPU or horizontal workers) to handle real disaster-scale load
Frontend is a single 305-line static HTML file — the actual public-facing UI is minimal/thin compared to the backend's sophistication and would need real frontend work for broad public use
nodes.mergePotential: This is a strong, largely self-contained flagship candidate rather than a merge target — other simpler people-finder or shelter-mapping projects in the hackathon could integrate as data sources/importers (the /encontrados/importar bulk-import endpoint is explicitly designed for this) rather than this project merging into something else.

diffusion.angle: Lead with the human story: a working facial-recognition tool, live today at vzlaencuentra.com, that can reunite Venezuelan families separated by the earthquake — upload one photo and get matched against everyone registered as found. Pair the live demo link with the Instagram reel to show it in action.
gaps: No confirmed real-user/real-match testimonial yet (a testimonios feature exists but needs a live success story for the post)
Could add a screenshot carousel of the actual search/upload flow for a static social post in addition to the video

findings.title: Unauthenticated bulk PII scraping via the text-search endpoints (phones, locations, minors' names)
Unauthenticated write lets anyone hijack a found person's live location and contact phone
Default admin password 'reencuentros2026' was hardcoded in source and is now permanently visible in git history
No rate limiting anywhere in the API
Uploaded photo/testimonio content type is trusted from the client header, not verified, and the standalone nginx config drops security headers on the /fotos/ static path

security.reauditNote: First-time audit; no prior security-tracking issue exists (only an unrelated, closed functional bug about local-photo persistence in Docker Compose, issue #4). The codebase shows real security-conscious effort — parametrized SQL everywhere, bcrypt+JWT admin auth applied consistently via `dependencies=[Depends(get_current_admin)]` on every admin/state-changing-admin route, a well-built SSRF guard for the image-import endpoint, and a previously-committed default admin password that was already found and removed from source (commit 0d868da). However, two public, unauthenticated endpoints undercut the app's own privacy model: a text-search endpoint that lets anyone paginate through and dump every missing/found person's phone number and location with a single wildcard query, and a public write endpoint that lets anyone overwrite a found person's live location/contact phone. Both are exploitable today with a single unauthenticated HTTP request.

nodes.name: zerena
nodes.type: web app (PWA)
nodes.summary: Zerena is a Spanish-language offline-capable PWA delivering Psychological First Aid (PAP) content for Venezuelan earthquake survivors, built with TanStack Start + Supabase, deployed via Lovable Cloud. It has real, well-…
nodes.onePitch: Zerena: primeros auxilios psicológicos offline y anónimos en tu bolsillo para cuando tiembla la tierra en Venezuela — sin señal, sin registro, sin juicio.

stack.orm: Supabase JS client (postgREST) for both client-side (anon key, RLS-scoped) and server-side (service role key for admin ops); no ORM, raw table queries via supabase-js
stack.pattern: Full-stack React meta-framework (TanStack Start) with file-based routing and server functions; Supabase as BaaS for auth/db/RLS

redFlags: .env file committed to git (contains Supabase project URL and anon key; not catastrophic but poor hygiene and should be rotated/removed from history)
No automated tests or CI pipeline despite handling sensitive support-request data
No visible rate-limiting/anti-abuse controls on public anonymous insert endpoints (support_requests, network_signups), risking spam or abuse of a crisis-support form
nodes.mergePotential: Could integrate with a shelter-mapping or people-finder platform as its mental-health support module, or share its volunteer/professional signup network with other aid-logistics projects; its RLS+defense-in-depth Supabase pattern is a good reference for other Supabase-based hackathon entries.

diffusion.angle: 'La app que te acompaña cuando tiembla la tierra y no hay señal': an offline, anonymous psychological first-aid PWA for Venezuelan earthquake survivors — install it from the browser, no signup, works without internet, with real content for panic, grief, and supporting others (children, elderly, people waiting for missing loved ones).
gaps: No confirmed real_users/usage evidence yet (0 GitHub stars, brand-new repo, no testimonials)
No rate-limiting/anti-abuse on public anonymous forms, a risk if promoted at scale
env file with Supabase keys committed to git history (hygiene issue, low severity since anon key only)
Screenshots not independently verified in this pass (only text-fetch of live site)

findings.title: session_notes table + its role-based RLS exist only as an untracked, hand-applied change on the live DB (schema drift, unauditable)
anon/authenticated retain default SELECT grants on PII tables; only RLS (no policy) currently blocks reads
Case-mutation server functions skip the per-case access check used by session notes, letting any staff role alter any case
Public support_requests INSERT allows the submitter to set workflow-control fields (status, assigned_to)
Supabase anon/publishable key and project URL committed in .env

security.reauditNote: First-time audit. No prior tracking issue exists (gh issue list returns empty). The dev history shows genuine security-hardening effort (IDOR fix on session notes with assertCaseAccess, REVOKE EXECUTE on SECURITY DEFINER functions, RLS-gated support_requests/user_roles), and live probing confirms anon cannot currently read support_requests, contact_messages, or session_notes (session_notes correctly returns 401 permission-denied). Remaining gaps are defense-in-depth/process issues rather than live PII leaks: the session_notes table and its RLS were applied by hand directly on the Supabase DB and never committed as a migration (admitted in commit bf60433), so the repo cannot reproduce/verify the protections for the most sensitive data (therapy notes on earthquake survivors); several tables still carry Supabase's default anon/authenticated SELECT grant that was never explicitly revoked, leaving RLS as the sole safety net; and three case-mutation server functions (urgency/status/assignment) skip the per-case assertCaseAccess check that session notes already use, so any staff account (even the lowest 'voluntario' role) can alter any case, not just its own.

nodes.name: GeoResponde
nodes.type: web app (disaster-response geospatial situation room: map dashboard + people-finder search + damage-report submission gateway)
nodes.summary: GeoResponde is a real, well-architected open-source geospatial disaster response platform for Venezuela: a React/MapLibre map visualizing earthquakes, faults, Copernicus/NASA damage layers, hospitals and citizen reports…
nodes.onePitch: GeoResponde federa en un solo mapa las decenas de plataformas de personas desaparecidas y daños del terremoto de Venezuela, para que nadie tenga que buscar sitio por sitio.

backend: custom Remix turbo-stream deserializer for scraping
database: none (no persistent DB; reads static GeoJSON/JSON catalog files, proxies live third-party APIs)
stack.orm: none — no database; backend proxies third-party HTTP APIs/scrapes HTML/turbo-stream responses and serves static GeoJSON files from public/data
stack.pattern: monorepo with adapter/gateway pattern: frontend SPA + Fastify backend 'Provider Gateway' that routes search/submit requests to per-source adapter classes implementing a common BaseAdapter interface

redFlags: Backend has no authentication or rate limiting on public search/submit endpoints
Debug/inspect endpoint (/api/dev/inspect/venezuelatebusca) exposed unconditionally in production server code
BuildingDamageForm hardcodes http://127.0.0.1:3001 instead of using VITE_API_URL, likely broken in the deployed environment
Core functionality depends on scraping undocumented third-party internal APIs (Remix turbo-stream payloads, unofficial terremotovenezuela.com API) that can break without notice
No database/persistence layer — user-submitted reports are only forwarded to a third-party API with no local record or retry/audit trail
Single contributor, project only 2-3 days old at time of review (created 2026-06-29, pushed 2026-07-01)
nodes.mergePotential: High potential to merge with people-finder/shelter-mapping or aid-logistics projects since it already implements a generic adapter/gateway pattern for federating third-party humanitarian data sources — other teams' datasets could be plugged in as new adapters or catalog entries with relatively low integration cost.

diffusion.angle: Position it as open infrastructure/interoperability play: 'one map to search across every missing-persons and damage-report site created after the Venezuela earthquake' — appeals to both civic-tech developers (adapter/SDK story) and NGOs/donors (unified situation room) rather than as a finished consumer product.
gaps: No demo video and no screenshots exist to show the actual map/search UI working, so there is nothing visual to post — WebFetch could not even render the JS app to confirm the UI looks good

Core building-damage submission form is hardcoded to a localhost URL and likely fails silently in production, which would embarrass the project if promoted right now and a viewer tries to submit a report

Backend has no auth/rate-limiting and depends on scraping undocumented third-party internal APIs (Remix turbo-stream payloads) that can break without notice, making the demo fragile for a live audience
Single contributor, project is only 2-3 days old — no evidence yet of real users, adoption by any humanitarian org, or other providers plugging into the federation SDK
No persistence layer, so any user-submitted report during a demo has no audit trail if the third-party forward fails

findings.title: Committed dump of trapped/missing-person names, home addresses, and coordinates from a real earthquake, publicly readable in the repo
Unauthenticated, unrate-limited submission proxy (POST /api/submit) forwards straight to a partner's live crisis-reporting API with open CORS
Unauthenticated debug/diagnostic endpoint left reachable in the deployed gateway

security.reauditNote: First-time audit; no prior security tracking issue exists on GeoResponde/georesponde. The most severe finding is a committed, currently-live PII dump of earthquake victims' names/addresses scraped from a partner API, plus an unauthenticated, unrate-limited proxy endpoint that forwards to a partner's live crisis-reporting API. Nothing has been fixed yet since these are all present at HEAD (da73ef8).
